news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking scientific discoveries in light and laser physics, remarkable AI advancements in mathematics, and innovative progress toward developing smart cities focused on human-centric solutions.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des découvertes scientifiques révolutionnaires dans le domaine de la physique de la lumière et du laser, des avancées remarquables de l'intelligence artificielle en mathématiques, ainsi que des progrès innovants dans le développement de villes intelligentes axées sur des solutions centrées sur l'humain.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan descubrimientos científicos revolucionarios en el campo de la física de la luz y el láser, avances notables de la inteligencia artificial en matemáticas y progresos innovadores en el desarrollo de ciudades inteligentes centradas en soluciones orientadas al ser humano.

articles.title: Scientists discover strange “narwhal” waves that trap light beyond known limits
articles.summary: Physicists at Peking University have uncovered a new way to confine light far beyond conventional limits — without relying on metals and their inherent energy dissipation. By formulating the singular dispersion equation, the team discovered narwhal-shaped wavefunctions that trap light at deep-subwavelength volumes in purely dielectric materials. The advance, dubbed singulonics, could pave the way for ultra-efficient photonic chips, new quantum technologies, and imaging tools with unprecedented resolution.
articles.content:
For decades, shrinking photonic devices has been far more difficult than miniaturizing electronic components. The challenge comes down to physics. Light cannot easily be confined into extremely small spaces because the uncertainty principle links its confinement to its wavelength. In visible and near infrared light, that wavelength can be up to a thousand times larger than the de Broglie wavelength used in electronic circuits. As a result, photonic chips have remained relatively bulky, and optical imaging systems have faced strict resolution limits.

Scientists previously explored plasmonics as a possible workaround. That approach uses metals to squeeze light into spaces smaller than its wavelength. However, metals generate significant heat through energy dissipation, creating a major obstacle for efficient and scalable photonic technologies.

In 2024, researchers led by Ren-Min Ma at Peking University in China introduced a major breakthrough [Nature 632, 287-293 (2024)]. The team developed what they call the singular dispersion equation, a new theoretical framework showing that light can be confined to extraordinarily small scales using lossless dielectric materials instead of metals. Because the method relies entirely on dielectrics, it avoids the heat losses that have limited plasmonic systems and could help pave the way for compact, energy efficient photonic devices.

Discovery of "Narwhal-Shaped" Wavefunctions

In a newly published paper in eLight, the same research team explains the origin of this extreme light confinement. According to the researchers, it arises from an entirely new class of electromagnetic eigenmodes known as narwhal-shaped wavefunctions.

These unusual modes combine two important behaviors. Near the singularity, the electromagnetic field experiences local power-law enhancement. At larger distances, the field rapidly fades through global exponential decay. Together, these properties allow light to become concentrated and compressed far beyond traditional physical limits.

Using this concept, the team designed and experimentally demonstrated a three dimensional singular dielectric resonator capable of confining light below the diffraction limit in all three spatial dimensions.

Record-Breaking Light Confinement

The researchers used near-field scanning measurements to directly observe the narwhal-shaped wavefunctions in action. Their measurements clearly showed the predicted power-law growth close to the singularity as well as the exponential decay farther away.

The experimental observations closely matched both theoretical predictions and full 3D simulations. The system achieved an ultrasmall mode volume of just 5 × 10-7 λ3, representing an extraordinary level of light confinement.

A New Type of Optical Microscope

The team also used the extreme localization of the narwhal-shaped wavefunctions to create a new near-field scanning optical microscopy technique called the singular optical microscope.

By exciting the eigenmodes of singular dielectric cavities, the microscope generates highly localized electromagnetic fields. Tiny changes in nearby structures cause measurable resonance shifts, allowing the system to detect extremely fine details.

The researchers demonstrated an unprecedented spatial resolution of λ/1000 and successfully imaged deep-subwavelength patterns, including the letters "PKU" and "SFM."

The Rise of "Singulonics"

The study shows that the singular dispersion equation produces narwhal-shaped wavefunctions capable of trapping light at remarkably small scales within lossless dielectric materials.

The researchers say this discovery forms the foundation of what they call singulonics, a new nanophotonic framework focused on controlling and confining light far below conventional limits without energy dissipation. The advance could support ultra efficient information processing technologies, create new opportunities in quantum optics, and expand the capabilities of super resolution imaging.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in light confinement using lossless dielectric materials, overcoming longstanding physical limits and heat dissipation issues in photonic devices. This advance has broad implications for ultra-efficient photonic chips, quantum technologies, and super-resolution imaging, offering tangible benefits for technology and society at large. The story is focused, detailed, and describes a novel discovery with clear real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover new way to trap light using ‘narwhal’ wave patterns
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, scientists have tried to make photonic devices smaller, but light is hard to squeeze into tiny spaces. Traditional methods using metals cause heat problems, limiting how small and efficient these devices can be.

**What happened** – Researchers at Peking University in China found a new way to trap light in very small spaces using special wave patterns shaped like narwhals, a type of whale. They created a new equation called the singular dispersion equation that explains how light can be confined inside materials that do not lose energy as heat, called dielectrics. They built a 3D device that traps light smaller than ever before and took pictures showing this effect clearly.

**Impact** – This discovery means photonic devices can become much smaller and work without overheating. It also led to a new type of microscope that can see details 1,000 times smaller than the wavelength of light, far beyond what was possible before. This can improve technologies like ultra-fast computer chips, quantum computers, and super-detailed imaging.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore singulonics, the new field based on these narwhal wave patterns, to develop better photonic devices and new quantum technologies. They will also work on improving the new microscope and finding more ways to use this light-trapping method in real-world applications.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new way to trap light using ‘narwhal-shaped’ waves in energy-efficient materials could transform future photonic devices and imaging technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent une nouvelle façon de piéger la lumière grâce à des ondes en forme de « narval »
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les scientifiques essaient de réduire la taille des dispositifs photoniques, mais la lumière est difficile à confiner dans de petits espaces. Les méthodes traditionnelles utilisant des métaux causent des problèmes de chaleur, limitant la taille et l’efficacité de ces dispositifs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Pékin en Chine ont trouvé une nouvelle manière de piéger la lumière dans des espaces très petits grâce à des motifs d’ondes en forme de narval, un type de baleine. Ils ont créé une nouvelle équation appelée équation de dispersion singulière qui explique comment la lumière peut être confinée dans des matériaux sans perte d’énergie, appelés diélectriques. Ils ont construit un dispositif 3D qui piège la lumière plus petit que jamais et ont pris des images montrant clairement cet effet.

**Impact** – Cette découverte signifie que les dispositifs photoniques peuvent devenir beaucoup plus petits et fonctionner sans surchauffe. Elle a aussi permis de créer un nouveau type de microscope capable de voir des détails 1 000 fois plus petits que la longueur d’onde de la lumière, bien au-delà des possibilités précédentes. Cela peut améliorer des technologies comme les puces ultra-rapides, les ordinateurs quantiques et l’imagerie super-détaillée.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont explorer la singulonique, le nouveau domaine basé sur ces ondes en forme de narval, pour développer de meilleurs dispositifs photoniques et des technologies quantiques. Ils vont aussi améliorer le nouveau microscope et trouver d’autres façons d’utiliser cette méthode de piégeage de la lumière dans des applications concrètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode pour piéger la lumière grâce à des ondes « en forme de narval » dans des matériaux économes en énergie pourrait transformer les dispositifs photoniques et la technologie d’imagerie du futur.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren nueva forma de atrapar la luz usando ondas con forma de ‘narval’
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, los científicos han intentado hacer dispositivos fotónicos más pequeños, pero la luz es difícil de encerrar en espacios muy pequeños. Los métodos tradicionales que usan metales generan calor, limitando el tamaño y la eficiencia de estos dispositivos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Pekín en China encontraron una nueva manera de atrapar la luz en espacios muy pequeños usando patrones de ondas con forma de narval, un tipo de ballena. Crearon una nueva ecuación llamada ecuación de dispersión singular que explica cómo la luz puede ser confinada dentro de materiales que no pierden energía como calor, llamados dieléctricos. Construyeron un dispositivo 3D que atrapa la luz en un volumen más pequeño que nunca y tomaron imágenes que muestran claramente este efecto.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento significa que los dispositivos fotónicos pueden ser mucho más pequeños y funcionar sin sobrecalentarse. También llevó a un nuevo tipo de microscopio capaz de ver detalles 1,000 veces más pequeños que la longitud de onda de la luz, mucho más allá de lo que se podía antes. Esto puede mejorar tecnologías como chips ultrarrápidos, computadoras cuánticas y técnicas de imagen súper detalladas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán la singulónica, el nuevo campo basado en estos patrones de ondas en forma de narval, para desarrollar mejores dispositivos fotónicos y nuevas tecnologías cuánticas. También trabajarán en mejorar el nuevo microscopio y encontrar más formas de usar este método para atrapar la luz en aplicaciones reales.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva forma de atrapar la luz usando ondas con forma de ‘narval’ en materiales que no pierden energía podría transformar los dispositivos fotónicos y la tecnología de imagen del futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, translucent narwhal-shaped wave gracefully emerging from a smooth, crystalline dielectric resonator, with delicate, swirling light patterns symbolizing confined electromagnetic fields tightly coiled near its singular horn and softly fading outward, set against a simple, softly colored abstract background evoking advanced nanophotonic innovation.

articles.title: Mathematicians stunned by AI's biggest breakthrough in mathematics yet
articles.summary: Artificial intelligence built by OpenAI has cracked a decades-old conjecture by Paul Erdős, which mathematicians have hailed as a monumental moment for AI in mathematics
articles.content:
Artificial intelligence built by OpenAI has cracked a decades-old conjecture by Paul Erdős, which mathematicians have hailed as a monumental moment for AI in mathematics

The planar unit distance problem is about how many equal-sized lines you can draw that connect dots on an infinite sheet of paper Noga Alon et al. 2026, Open AI

An 80-year-old maths conjecture that has eluded the world’s greatest mathematicians has been cracked by an artificial intelligence model built by OpenAI. The result has stunned experts and is being hailed as a seismic moment for AI’s mathematical ability.

“This is a problem that I didn’t expect to see solved in my lifetime,” says Misha Rudnev at the University of Bristol, UK. “It’s absolutely a bomb.”

Tim Gowers at the University of Cambridge wrote that the solution is “a milestone in AI mathematics” in a blog post accompanying the work. “If a human had written the paper and submitted it to the Annals of Mathematics and I had been asked for a quick opinion, I would have recommended acceptance without any hesitation. No previous AI-generated proof has come close to that.”

Twentieth-century mathematician Paul Erdős considered the puzzle, known as the planar unit distance problem, as his “most striking contribution to geometry”, because it was seemingly simple to explain but deeply complex to answer. He asked: if you take an infinite-sized piece of paper and draw a number of dots in a pattern of your choice, what is the maximum number of equal-sized lines you can draw between these dots?

Erdős conjectured that the patterns that yielded the most connections were points arranged in a grid, meaning the maximum number of connections would be only slightly higher than the number of points themselves. Successive attempts to prove that this really is the upper limit, or find a different arrangement of points that might lead to many more connections, yielded only small successes. The most recent improvement to Erdős’s conjecture was more than 40 years ago.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

Now, a model from OpenAI has found that Erdős was significantly wrong, and that you can arrange points in less symmetric patterns that can yield a far greater number of pairs.

“My immediate reaction was disbelief,” says Will Sawin at Princeton University. “I thought the way that it was trying to solve it wouldn’t work, but then I looked at it more and I convinced myself that it does work. I pretty quickly became convinced this is the most significant achievement by AI in mathematics so far.”

OpenAI hasn’t said exactly how the model differs from publicly available AIs or how it was trained, but the firm’s researchers have publicly commented that the model is “general purpose” and wasn’t trained “with the goal of doing math research”.

The AI borrowed a technique from algebraic number theory to construct vast lattices in much higher dimensions than the two of a plane. Once it had identified and built these more complex shapes, it then collapsed them down to two dimensions, producing a shadow of the higher-dimensional shapes.

“The counterexample discovered by the AI is complex, and although the ideas to produce it were already in the literature, it certainly takes some ingenuity to put them together,” says Kevin Buzzard at Imperial College London.

While the result is impressive, it is also partly a consequence of the fact that mathematicians didn’t even consider that Erdős’s original conjecture may have been false, says Samuel Mansfield at the University of Manchester. UK. Even if mathematicians did experiment with disproving it, very few geometry specialists would have then been knowledgeable enough in advanced number theory to do so. “This is something that requires you to know a lot about multiple areas,” he says. “In retrospect, it’s maybe not so surprising. This seems to be what an AI would absolutely be good at doing.”

The main appeal of the problem was the “pure intellectual challenge”, says Rudnev, and it may not have any particular ramifications for other outstanding problems, but it has already sparked some further work. After seeing the proof, Sawin used the technique that the AI had discovered to produce a slightly improved, higher number for how many points could be joined together.

“Like many other AI breakthroughs, it did not take humans long at all to internalise, understand and generalise the arguments,” says Buzzard. “One can contrast this with some human breakthroughs which have taken the community months or years to validate.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough where an AI developed by OpenAI solved an 80-year-old mathematical conjecture by Paul Erdős, a problem that had eluded top mathematicians for decades. This achievement demonstrates a major advancement in AI's capability to contribute to complex mathematical research, with broad implications for the future of technology and scientific discovery. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the real-world impact of AI in advancing human knowledge.
articles.personality_title: AI solves 80-year-old math puzzle once thought unsolvable
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For over 80 years, mathematicians have tried to solve a puzzle called the planar unit distance problem. It asks how many equal-length lines can connect dots arranged on an infinite flat surface. The problem was first studied by Paul Erdős, a famous mathematician, who guessed that points arranged in a grid gave the most connections, but no one could prove it.

**What happened** – An artificial intelligence (AI) model created by OpenAI recently found a surprising answer. Instead of grids, the AI discovered new, less regular patterns that allow many more connections between points. Experts from top universities have checked the AI’s work and say it is correct. This is the first time AI has solved such a difficult and long-standing math problem.

**Impact** – This breakthrough shows AI can now help solve complex math questions that have challenged humans for decades. The AI used ideas from advanced math fields and combined them in ways people hadn’t tried before. It also helped mathematicians understand and improve the solution quickly. This marks an important step in using AI not just for calculations but for real research.

**What's next step** – Researchers are now studying the AI’s methods to see how they can be applied to other math problems. The AI’s solution has already inspired new discoveries and could lead to faster progress in several areas of mathematics. OpenAI and other scientists will likely continue developing AI tools to assist with more challenging research in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – An AI built by OpenAI has solved a famous 80-year-old math problem, opening new doors for AI-assisted discoveries in mathematics.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une IA résout un problème mathématique vieux de 80 ans autrefois insoluble
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plus de 80 ans, les mathématiciens essaient de résoudre un casse-tête appelé le problème de la distance unitaire planaire. Il s’agit de savoir combien de lignes de même longueur peuvent relier des points disposés sur une surface plane infinie. Ce problème a été étudié pour la première fois par Paul Erdős, un mathématicien célèbre, qui pensait que les points en grille donnaient le plus de connexions, mais personne n’a pu le prouver.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un modèle d’intelligence artificielle (IA) créé par OpenAI a récemment trouvé une réponse surprenante. Au lieu de grilles, l’IA a découvert de nouveaux motifs moins réguliers permettant bien plus de connexions entre les points. Des experts des meilleures universités ont vérifié le travail de l’IA et confirment qu’il est correct. C’est la première fois qu’une IA résout un problème mathématique aussi difficile et ancien.

**Impact** – Cette avancée montre que l’IA peut désormais aider à résoudre des questions mathématiques complexes qui défiaient les humains depuis des décennies. L’IA a utilisé des idées issues de domaines mathématiques avancés et les a combinées de manière inédite. Elle a aussi aidé les mathématiciens à comprendre et améliorer la solution rapidement. C’est une étape importante pour utiliser l’IA non seulement pour calculer, mais aussi pour la recherche.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs étudient maintenant les méthodes de l’IA pour voir comment elles peuvent s’appliquer à d’autres problèmes mathématiques. La solution de l’IA a déjà inspiré de nouvelles découvertes et pourrait accélérer le progrès dans plusieurs domaines des mathématiques. OpenAI et d’autres scientifiques vont probablement continuer à développer des outils d’IA pour aider à des recherches plus complexes.

**Une phrase clé** – Une IA développée par OpenAI a résolu un célèbre problème mathématique vieux de 80 ans, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour les découvertes assistées par l’IA en mathématiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Una IA resuelve un problema matemático de 80 años antes considerado imposible
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de 80 años, los matemáticos han intentado resolver un problema llamado el problema de la distancia unitaria planar. Pregunta cuántas líneas iguales se pueden dibujar conectando puntos en una superficie plana infinita. El problema fue estudiado primero por Paul Erdős, un famoso matemático, quien pensó que los puntos en una cuadrícula daban las mayores conexiones, pero nadie pudo probarlo.

**Qué pasó** – Un modelo de inteligencia artificial (IA) creado por OpenAI encontró recientemente una respuesta sorprendente. En lugar de cuadrículas, la IA descubrió nuevos patrones menos regulares que permiten muchas más conexiones entre puntos. Expertos de universidades importantes revisaron el trabajo de la IA y dijeron que es correcto. Es la primera vez que una IA resuelve un problema matemático tan difícil y antiguo.

**Impacto** – Este avance muestra que la IA ahora puede ayudar a resolver preguntas matemáticas complejas que han desafiado a humanos durante décadas. La IA usó ideas de campos matemáticos avanzados y las combinó de formas que la gente no había intentado antes. También ayudó a los matemáticos a entender y mejorar la solución rápidamente. Esto es un paso importante para usar la IA no solo para cálculos, sino para investigación real.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están estudiando los métodos de la IA para ver cómo aplicarlos a otros problemas matemáticos. La solución de la IA ya ha inspirado nuevos descubrimientos y podría acelerar el progreso en varias áreas de las matemáticas. OpenAI y otros científicos probablemente seguirán desarrollando herramientas de IA para ayudar con investigaciones más difíciles.

**Una frase clave** – Una IA creada por OpenAI resolvió un famoso problema matemático de 80 años, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para descubrimientos asistidos por IA en matemáticas.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, glowing lattice of interconnected dots and lines floats above an open, infinite sheet of paper, with delicate shadows revealing complex higher-dimensional shapes folding down into the plane, all rendered in soft, natural hues with warm light highlighting the breakthrough and harmony between artificial intelligence and deep mathematical insight.

articles.title: Building the smart city: Promise, pitfalls and the people at its heart
articles.summary: From AI-powered transit systems to digital twins and flood-proof parks, cities are embracing technology at an unprecedented scale. But as innovation accelerates, experts warn that inclusion, trust and security will determine who truly benefits.
articles.content:
At a sprawling urban expo in Baku, visitors paused before giant digital screens flickering with flood simulations, subway control systems and virtual replicas of entire neighbourhoods. Across the space, cities offered a vision of urban life reshaped by artificial intelligence, digital infrastructure and real-time data – a glimpse of what many governments now call the “city of the future.”

A smart city in practice

Few cities illustrate the scale of smart-city ambition quite like Shanghai. At the China Pavilion at the thirteenth session of the World Urban Forum (WUF13) in Baku, officials showcased how the city is using big data and artificial intelligence to build what it calls a “Unified Management with One Network” platform – a vast system bringing together transportation, infrastructure, emergency response and public services.

Shanghai has also sought to narrow the digital divide. Through its “One-stop Government Service” portal, residents can access more than 3,500 public services online.

The city’s transportation system offers another glimpse into the future. The Shanghai Metro – one of the world’s largest, spanning more than 800 kilometres and over 400 stations – relies on AI-driven monitoring, 5G-enabled communications and predictive weather-control technologies to improve safety and reduce disruptions.

Several lines already operate fully automated, driverless trains. AI systems monitor tunnels for cracks, water leaks and structural weaknesses, while vast streams of passenger data are analysed in real time to prevent disruptions across the network.

Perhaps the most striking example is the Lingang Starry Sky Sponge Park. Covering 54 hectares, it combines a cosmic-themed landscape with advanced flood management infrastructure. Permeable pavements, underground filtration systems and smart drainage allow the park to absorb, clean and reuse up to 15,000 cubic metres of rainwater each day.

UN News

Putting people at the centre

Anacláudia Rossbach, Executive Director of UN-Habitat, which is hosting the forum alongside Azerbaijan, warned that cities are confronting multiple crises, from housing shortages and informal settlements to climate shocks and rising inequality.

“We really need to max out the technologies that we have available,” she said, noting that cities remain on the front lines of climate change, conflict and rapid urbanization.

But, she stressed, technology alone is not enough.

“The most important thing of our work is that smart cities really need to be people-centered,” she said, adding that human rights, inclusion and equity must remain at the heart of digital transformation.

She also pointed to the risks of digital exclusion. During the COVID-19 pandemic, many residents of informal settlements were cut off from education, services and remote work due to a lack of connectivity and digital access.

© Bogota Mayor's Office/Cristia

Who really benefits?

Yet even as cities embrace artificial intelligence and digital infrastructure, some researchers caution against focusing too heavily on technology itself.

“Most people understand a smart city as being technology-driven,” Gynna Millan, an architect and urban researcher from Colombia, told us. She warned that technology can either empower people or become “a tool for control,” depending on how it is used.

For many cities in the Global South – particularly informal settlements that often lack basic infrastructure or reliable internet – the question is not simply whether advanced technology exists, she said, but whether it genuinely improves daily life.

AI arrives at city hall

Artificial intelligence is rapidly moving from pilot projects into everyday urban governance. “AI is no longer just a buzzword,” said Dmitri Atov, an adviser to the Moscow city government. “AI is a game changer for cities.”

Yining Zhao, a project officer at the UN International Telecommunication Union (ITU), outlined five technological trends shaping urban life:

‘World models’ — AI systems that simulate how cities and infrastructure behave in real-world conditions.

— AI systems that simulate how cities and infrastructure behave in real-world conditions. ‘Agentic AI’ — autonomous digital agents capable of planning and acting independently.

— autonomous digital agents capable of planning and acting independently. AI in the physical world — through robots, drones and intelligent machines.

— through robots, drones and intelligent machines. Immersive virtual environments — digital worlds for work, learning and collaboration.

— digital worlds for work, learning and collaboration. ‘Digital twins’ — virtual city replicas used to test scenarios in real time.

Moscow already operates one of the world’s largest digital-twin systems. Updated twice a year using aerial photography, it contains more than 9,000 analytical layers used to model traffic, infrastructure and future development.

The risks beneath the surface

As cities become more connected, they also grow more vulnerable. In Azerbaijan, authorities say cybersecurity is becoming central to urban development as smart technologies expand.

“Cyberattacks are not just a technical issue,” Aytaj Khalafli, a representative of the country’s state cybersecurity agency, told us. Cybersecurity, she said, must be built into urbanization “from the foundation stage.”

Her agency protects critical infrastructure, monitors threats and conducts penetration tests across government systems.

“If an attack happens, chaos can emerge in the country,” Ms. Khalafli warned, noting that transport systems have already faced cyber incidents.

Throughout discussions in Baku, UN experts stressed that trust, transparency and security must evolve alongside innovation.

“Trust today is no longer just a luxury – it’s infrastructure,” said Nicholas You, Executive Director of the Guangzhou Institute for Urban Innovation (China).

© Unsplash/Oscar Daniel Rangel

Smart, but for whom?

The future of smart cities, UN officials insist, will not be decided by who has the biggest data centres, the most sensors or the fastest AI systems.

“‘Smart’ does not always mean introducing digital technologies or even innovation. Sometimes it is about good planning, setting priorities, and understanding how infrastructure is interconnected,” said Dmitry Maryasin of the UN Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE).

As examples, he pointed to city greening projects, the conversion of highways into public spaces, and the growth of “active mobility,” where priority is given to pedestrians and cyclists.

In the end, the question may be less about how smart cities can become, and more about who they are designed to serve.

UN News is in Baku covering the Summit throughout the week. Follow along here.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes significant, real-world advancements in smart city technologies with broad societal impact, including AI-driven urban management, flood-proof parks, and inclusive digital services. It highlights meaningful progress in urban innovation that can improve quality of life for large populations, while also addressing challenges like digital inclusion and cybersecurity. The focus on people-centered design and equitable benefits adds substance and relevance beyond mere technological hype.
articles.personality_title: How cities like Shanghai use AI and smart design to improve daily life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Cities around the world are using new technologies like artificial intelligence (AI) and digital tools to become “smart cities.” These places aim to make life easier, safer, and more efficient for their residents by managing things like transport, public services, and the environment with advanced systems.

**What happened**
At a big urban forum in Baku, experts shared how cities such as Shanghai are already putting these ideas into action. Shanghai has created a large system that connects transport, emergency services, and public services using AI and big data. Its metro system uses AI to monitor safety and run driverless trains. The city also built a large park with smart flood-control features that can collect and reuse rainwater. Other cities like Moscow are using “digital twins,” virtual copies of the city, to plan and manage infrastructure.

**Impact**
These examples show how technology can help cities work better and protect people from problems like floods or transport delays. But experts also warn that technology must include everyone to be fair. For example, during the COVID-19 pandemic, people without internet access missed out on important services. Cities need to focus on being people-centered, making sure new tools benefit all residents and protect their rights.

**What's next step**
Cities will continue to develop and use AI and digital tools, but they must also strengthen cybersecurity to keep systems safe from attacks. Experts say trust and fairness should guide future smart city projects. This means planning with people’s needs in mind, not just adding more technology.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Smart cities use AI and digital tools to improve urban life, but success depends on including everyone and keeping systems secure.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment des villes comme Shanghai utilisent l’IA et la conception intelligente pour améliorer la vie quotidienne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Dans le monde entier, les villes utilisent de nouvelles technologies comme l’intelligence artificielle (IA) et des outils numériques pour devenir des « villes intelligentes ». Ces lieux visent à rendre la vie plus facile, plus sûre et plus efficace pour leurs habitants en gérant les transports, les services publics et l’environnement grâce à des systèmes avancés.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Lors d’un grand forum urbain à Bakou, des experts ont présenté comment des villes comme Shanghai mettent déjà ces idées en pratique. Shanghai a créé un grand système qui relie les transports, les services d’urgence et les services publics grâce à l’IA et aux mégadonnées. Son métro utilise l’IA pour surveiller la sécurité et faire fonctionner des trains sans conducteur. La ville a aussi construit un grand parc avec des systèmes intelligents de gestion des inondations qui collectent et réutilisent l’eau de pluie. D’autres villes comme Moscou utilisent des « jumeaux numériques », des copies virtuelles de la ville, pour planifier et gérer les infrastructures.

**Impact**
Ces exemples montrent comment la technologie peut aider les villes à mieux fonctionner et à protéger les habitants contre des problèmes comme les inondations ou les retards dans les transports. Mais les experts avertissent aussi que la technologie doit inclure tout le monde pour être juste. Par exemple, pendant la pandémie de COVID-19, les personnes sans accès à Internet ont été exclues de services importants. Les villes doivent rester centrées sur les personnes, en veillant à ce que les nouveaux outils bénéficient à tous et protègent leurs droits.

**Prochaine étape**
Les villes continueront à développer et utiliser l’IA et les outils numériques, mais elles doivent aussi renforcer la cybersécurité pour protéger les systèmes contre les attaques. Les experts disent que la confiance et l’équité doivent guider les futurs projets de villes intelligentes. Cela signifie planifier en pensant aux besoins des habitants, pas seulement ajouter plus de technologie.

**En une phrase**
Les villes intelligentes utilisent l’IA et les outils numériques pour améliorer la vie urbaine, mais leur succès dépend d’une inclusion totale et de la sécurité des systèmes.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo ciudades como Shanghái usan IA y diseño inteligente para mejorar la vida diaria
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
En todo el mundo, las ciudades están usando nuevas tecnologías como la inteligencia artificial (IA) y herramientas digitales para convertirse en «ciudades inteligentes». Estas buscan facilitar, hacer más seguro y eficiente el día a día de sus habitantes, gestionando el transporte, los servicios públicos y el medio ambiente con sistemas avanzados.

**Qué pasó**
En un gran foro urbano en Bakú, expertos mostraron cómo ciudades como Shanghái ya están aplicando estas ideas. Shanghái creó un sistema grande que conecta transporte, servicios de emergencia y servicios públicos usando IA y grandes datos. Su metro usa IA para monitorear la seguridad y operar trenes sin conductor. La ciudad también construyó un parque con sistemas inteligentes para controlar inundaciones que recogen y reutilizan el agua de lluvia. Otras ciudades como Moscú usan «gemelos digitales», copias virtuales de la ciudad, para planificar y manejar la infraestructura.

**Impacto**
Estos ejemplos muestran cómo la tecnología puede ayudar a que las ciudades funcionen mejor y protejan a las personas de problemas como inundaciones o retrasos en el transporte. Pero los expertos advierten que la tecnología debe incluir a todos para ser justa. Por ejemplo, durante la pandemia de COVID-19, muchas personas sin acceso a internet quedaron fuera de servicios importantes. Las ciudades deben enfocarse en las personas, asegurando que las nuevas herramientas beneficien a todos y protejan sus derechos.

**Próximo paso**
Las ciudades seguirán desarrollando y usando IA y herramientas digitales, pero también deben fortalecer la ciberseguridad para proteger los sistemas de ataques. Los expertos dicen que la confianza y la equidad deben guiar los futuros proyectos de ciudades inteligentes. Esto significa planificar pensando en las necesidades de las personas, no solo en añadir más tecnología.

**Una frase resumen**
Las ciudades inteligentes usan IA y herramientas digitales para mejorar la vida urbana, pero su éxito depende de incluir a todos y mantener los sistemas seguros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an interconnected urban landscape featuring stylized digital screens displaying flood simulations and subway maps, a sprawling cosmic-themed park with permeable pavements and water flowing through smart drainage channels, and glowing abstract network lines weaving through buildings symbolizing AI-driven data and connectivity, all under a softly illuminated sky suggesting harmony between technology and people.

articles.title: Physicists finally solve the strange mystery of “breathing” lasers
articles.summary: Scientists have finally figured out how mysterious “breather” laser pulses work, solving a puzzle that has frustrated laser physicists for years. These unusual ultrafast lasers produce light pulses that rhythmically grow and shrink instead of staying steady, almost like they’re breathing.
articles.content:
An international team of researchers, including a scientist from Aston University, has developed a new mathematical framework that explains the strange behavior of so called "breather" laser pulses. The breakthrough unites two very different types of laser dynamics under a single model for the first time.

Ultrafast lasers generate incredibly short bursts of light that last only picoseconds or femtoseconds. These lasers are widely used in technologies such as eye surgery, biomedical imaging, advanced manufacturing, and precision materials processing. A deeper understanding of how these lasers behave could help scientists improve their stability and tailor them more effectively for specialized applications.

Inside an ultrafast laser, pulses of light travel repeatedly through a structure known as a laser cavity. Under certain conditions, these pulses can form stable wave packets called solitons. Unlike ordinary light pulses that gradually spread out, solitons maintain their shape as they move.

Most of the time, solitons behave in a steady and predictable way, producing regular pulses similar to a heartbeat. However, in "breather" lasers, the pulses continually change over time. They repeatedly grow and shrink during successive trips through the laser cavity, creating a rhythmic oscillation that resembles breathing. This behavior represents a non-equilibrium state in which the laser output constantly evolves instead of remaining stable.

Two Different Types of Laser "Breathing"

Previous experiments revealed two distinct forms of breathing behavior in these lasers.

When the laser operates above the minimum power needed to sustain pulse emission, known as the threshold, the solitons oscillate rapidly. In this regime, the breathing cycle repeats after only a few cavity roundtrips.

Below the threshold, the behavior becomes dramatically slower. The solitons may require hundreds or even thousands of roundtrips to complete a single breathing cycle.

Until now, researchers relied on two separate mathematical models to explain these different regimes. The new study changes that by showing that both behaviors can be described within one unified framework.

The work, which included Dr. Sonia Boscolo from the Aston Institute of Photonic Technologies, was published in Physical Review Letters in a paper titled "Unified model for breathing solitons in fiber lasers: Mechanisms across below- and above-threshold regimes."

A Unified Explanation for Complex Laser Dynamics

The researchers created a revised model that combines two important factors: the rapid evolution of light inside the laser cavity and the slower changes occurring in the laser's energy supply. By accounting for both processes together, the team demonstrated that the two forms of breathing are not separate phenomena but instead arise from related underlying physics.

Dr. Boscolo said:

"Above- and below-threshold breathing solitons show markedly different behaviors. Above-threshold breathers oscillate rapidly and can lock to the cavity, producing comb-like radiofrequency spectra and higher-order frequency-locked states, with characteristic sidebands in their optical spectrum. Below-threshold breathers evolve much more slowly, producing densely clustered radiofrequency spectra without strict commensurability, and without optical sidebands. Our new simulation accurately predicts both the fast and slow cycles in one go, something that was previously thought to be impossible with a single model.

"Our work introduces a revised discrete model that incorporates the slow dynamics of the laser gain medium while retaining the detailed cavity description. This unified framework accurately reproduces all experimentally observed behaviors in both regimes and reveals their underlying mechanisms: below-threshold breathing arises from Q-switching combined with soliton shaping, while above-threshold breathers are dominated by Kerr nonlinearity and dispersion.

"This discovery closes a long-standing gap in laser science and provides a vital tool for designing the next generation of light-based technologies."

Future Applications for Ultrafast Lasers

The researchers believe the new framework could become an important tool for engineers developing future optical systems. As demand grows for more powerful and dependable laser technologies, the model may help scientists predict complex laser behaviors more efficiently without relying on multiple disconnected simulations.

The team hopes the work will ultimately serve as a practical guide for designing the next generation of ultrafast lasers used in medicine, imaging, manufacturing, and other advanced technologies.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding complex laser dynamics, unifying previously separate models into one framework. This advancement has broad implications for improving ultrafast laser technologies widely used in medicine, imaging, and manufacturing, thus offering tangible benefits to society and various industries.
articles.personality_title: Scientists solve the mystery of breathing lasers with a new unified model
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ultrafast lasers create extremely short bursts of light and are used in many fields like eye surgery and manufacturing. Some of these lasers produce pulses that grow and shrink rhythmically, called "breather" lasers, which puzzled scientists for years.

**What happened** – An international research team, including Dr. Sonia Boscolo from Aston University, developed a new mathematical model that explains how these breathing laser pulses work. This model combines two different types of breathing behaviors seen in lasers above and below a certain power level, which were previously explained separately.

**Impact** – This unified model helps scientists understand the complex ways these laser pulses change over time. It shows that the different breathing behaviors come from related causes and predicts both fast and slow pulse cycles with one framework. This breakthrough fills a big gap in laser science and can improve how lasers are designed and used.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope this model will guide engineers in creating better ultrafast lasers. These improved lasers could be more stable and powerful, benefiting areas like medical treatments, imaging, and advanced manufacturing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new mathematical model now explains the puzzling breathing patterns of ultrafast lasers, helping improve future laser technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques résolvent le mystère des lasers « respirants » grâce à un modèle unifié
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les lasers ultrarapides produisent des éclairs de lumière très courts et sont utilisés dans des domaines comme la chirurgie oculaire et la fabrication. Certains de ces lasers émettent des impulsions qui grandissent et rétrécissent de manière rythmée, appelées lasers « respirants », un phénomène qui a intrigué les scientifiques pendant des années.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe internationale de chercheurs, incluant le Dr Sonia Boscolo de l’université d’Aston, a créé un nouveau modèle mathématique qui explique le fonctionnement de ces impulsions laser « respirantes ». Ce modèle réunit deux types de comportements observés au-dessus et en dessous d’un certain seuil de puissance, qui étaient auparavant expliqués séparément.

**Impact** – Ce modèle unifié aide à comprendre comment ces impulsions laser évoluent dans le temps. Il montre que les différents comportements de respiration ont des causes liées et peut prédire à la fois les cycles rapides et lents avec un seul cadre. Cette avancée comble une grande lacune en science des lasers et peut améliorer la conception et l’utilisation des lasers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent que ce modèle guidera les ingénieurs dans la création de lasers ultrarapides plus performants. Ces lasers améliorés pourraient être plus stables et puissants, au bénéfice de la médecine, de l’imagerie et de la fabrication avancée.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau modèle mathématique explique désormais les mystérieux comportements des lasers ultrarapides « respirants », facilitant l’amélioration des technologies laser futures.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos resuelven el misterio de los láseres "respirantes" con un modelo unificado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los láseres ultrarrápidos producen destellos de luz muy cortos y se usan en áreas como la cirugía ocular y la fabricación. Algunos de estos láseres emiten pulsos que crecen y se encogen rítmicamente, llamados láseres "respirantes", un fenómeno que ha desconcertado a los científicos durante años.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo internacional de investigadores, incluyendo a la Dra. Sonia Boscolo de la Universidad de Aston, desarrolló un nuevo modelo matemático que explica cómo funcionan estos pulsos láser "respirantes". Este modelo une dos tipos diferentes de comportamientos que ocurren por encima y por debajo de cierto nivel de potencia, que antes se explicaban por separado.

**Impacto** – Este modelo unificado ayuda a entender cómo cambian estos pulsos láser con el tiempo. Muestra que los diferentes tipos de respiración tienen causas relacionadas y puede predecir tanto ciclos rápidos como lentos con un solo marco. Este avance cierra una gran brecha en la ciencia del láser y puede mejorar el diseño y uso de los láseres.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que este modelo guíe a los ingenieros para crear láseres ultrarrápidos mejores. Estos láseres mejorados podrían ser más estables y potentes, beneficiando a la medicina, la imagen médica y la fabricación avanzada.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo modelo matemático explica los patrones de respiración de los láseres ultrarrápidos, ayudando a mejorar las tecnologías láser del futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of intertwined glowing light pulses gently expanding and contracting rhythmically within a softly illuminated, abstract fiber-optic loop, symbolizing the unified breathing cycles of ultrafast laser solitons, rendered in natural, muted tones with subtle gradients to evoke harmony and dynamic balance.
